text: PRECISION AND ERROR ANALYSIS 99

A series of measurements were made of the conductivities of several substances (Ar, He, $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ , Hg, and liquid and solid heat-transfer salt) over a wide range of conductivities $[0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ to $100 \times 10^{-3}$ W cm $^{-1}$ (°C) $^{-1}$ ] and temperatures (40 to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
The deviations of the results from published values averaged ±5%.
Most of the existing measurements for molten fluoride salt mixtures were made by members of the MSBR group at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) over 15 years

ago.2 To extend the scope of the previous measurements, we have developed an absolute, variable-gap technique to determine the thermal conductivity of fluoride salt mixtures in liquid and solid states at temperatures to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The development of an experimental apparatus is described, and experimental results are presented for the conductivities of several calibrating fluids: Ar, He, heat-transfer salt* (HTS), $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ , and Hg.

These fluids represent a very wide range of conductivities $[0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ to $100 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Wcm}^{-1}(\mathrm{C})^{-1}]$ which were measured over a large temperature range (40 to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
The line heat source consists of a wire (1 to 5 mils diam) placed axially in a cylinder (2 to 4 cm diam) filled with the specimen.
It is a common technique for determining conductivity of liquids and has been described in many publications.[3-5]

This technique<sup>7,8</sup> is similar to the transient hot-wire technique except in two respects: (1) a thin foil is substituted for the fine wire to provide a plane heat source instead of a line source, and (2) the temperature is measured with a front-wave-shearing laser interferometer.

This method<sup>9</sup> is based on the measurement of the steady-state change in resistance caused by electrical heating of a narrow bridge of the sample material which joins two larger bodies of the same material.
Most of the published results of this technique differ from the accepted values for thermal conductivity because the hydrodynamic and thermal entry lengths were not properly assessed.[10]
Considering the advantages of the method, it is surprising that only limited use has been made of the variable-gap technique.[12,14,15]

The $\mathbb{N}_{\mathrm{Ra}} = 1700$ criteria could lead to a gross overestimate of $\Delta \Gamma_{\mathrm{c}}$ for small specimen thicknesses.

Heat-transfer measurements made during Norden and Usmanov's studies showed the ratio of the effective conductivity to the true conductivity for water to be 1.10 at a specimen thickness of 0.15 cm and an $N_{\mathrm{Ra}}$ of only 260.

The apparatus described in the present report is designed to minimize the shunting error with specimens having estimated thermal conductivities in the range of 0.05 to 0.10 W cm $^{-1}$ (°C) $^{-1}$ .
The cell wall thickness and the ratio of cell diameter to sample thickness are optimized to reduce the heat shunted to less than 1% of the total heat flow in the absence of heat guards.

The cylindrical component was machined from three pieces of stainless steel type 304 and welded together as shown.

The lower section, which contains the molten salt, has holes drilled through the component for cooling air passages, and a sink heater which is a $1/8$ -in.-diam Calrod sheath-type element pressed into grooves machined in the lower section.
Figure 12 shows the location and designations of the thermocouples and heaters.
The cylinder is about $4$ in.
The main heater is constructed with 10-mil Pt-10% Rh wire, wound, embedded, and buried in a high-density $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ insulator.
Between these two heaters is a 1/16-in. gap containing three platinum-foil radiation shields.
The specifications of these tolerances insure that the parallelism of the two plate surfaces does not vary by more than 0.005 in.
The maximum edge-to-edge variation of the thickness in this case of 0.0017 in. demonstrates excellent conformity to the 0.005-in. tolerance.
The notation, description, and chronological appearance of these modifications are listed in Table 1.

The furnace consists of two 6-in.-ID × 8-in.-long individually controlled clamshell heaters of the embedded wire type.
The annular space between the heater and the 12.5-in.-OD water-cooled furnace shell was filled with Fiberfrax insulation.
This furnace is capable of raising the ambient temperature of the molten salt to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The fine adjustment extends the coarse Variac setting with the addition of $6\mathrm{V}$ of increased sensitivity $(0.038\mathrm{V})$ .
Specifications of these instruments are given in Table A-1 in Appendix A.
thermocouples or with ungrounded 1/16-in.-OD Pt vs Pt-10% Rh thermocouples; both are sheathed in 304 type stainless steel.

To minimize and cancel extraneous thermal emf's, each of these junctions is contained in a mineral-oil-filled glass tube inserted in a hole drilled into a high-purity copper block, $3 \times 2$ in. diameter, and the whole assembly is immersed in a distilled water ice bath.
Each thermocouple emf can be read by either a Vidar digital microvoltmeter, a Honeywell pen recorder, or a Leeds and Northrup K-5 potentiometer facility.
The specifications of these instruments are given in Table A-1, Appendix A.
After use, the corrosion products are removed by polishing with rotary stainless steel brushes, 600-grit cloth, and crocus cloth to restore the surfaces.
In assembling the apparatus, the parallelism of the plates is checked for the specified tolerance (0.005 in.), and the thermocouple readings at room temperature match within $< 0.2\mu V$ .

The purity of the specimens used in the determinations meet the ACS specifications for reagent-grade chemicals: $99.997\%$ pure argon, $99.995\%$ pure helium, doubly deionized and triple distilled water, triple distilled mercury, and reagent-grade $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ salts dried in vacuum.
The system is then outgassed by heating overnight at about $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The main heater is adjusted to obtain the required heat flux through the specimen (0.02 to $0.7 \, \text{W cm}^{-2}$ ), and the guard heaters are adjusted to maintain a close temperature balance between thermocouples three and five ( $< \pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ ) and thermocouples three and four ( $< \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ ) to minimize heat losses (see Fig.
After steady-state conditions are reached, the measurements are recorded; the criterion for the steady-state condition is a temperature drift of less than $1^{\circ}\text{C/hr}$ .
A number of gap spacings are used (0 to $0.4 \, \text{cm}$ ) depending on the linearity of the data.
The heat flux and average specimen temperature are kept constant for each gap spacing ( $> \pm 0.005 \, \text{W cm}^{-2}$ , $> \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ ).
The thermal conductivities of five materials were determined over a temperature range from 38 to $946^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for a total of 31 series of measurements.

Argon, helium, water, HTS, and mercury were selected to calibrate the apparatus because their conductivities are well established and cover a wide range of values: $0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ to $100 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W cm}^{-1}(\text{°C})^{-1}$ .
Nearly 350 measurements were made of the thermal resistance as a function of specimen thickness to obtain the 31 determinations of conductivity.
In addition, another 50 measurements of resistance vs thickness were made with the system evacuated to evaluate the surface emissivities.
The original and reduced data are presented in Tables B-1 to B-22 in Appendix B.
The data were reduced as indicated in the method of calculation in Chapter 2.

The experimental results for the thermal conductivity for the different calibration specimens used with the various apparatus models are presented in Table 2.

One value for the conductivity of solid HTS at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is $14\%$ larger than a published value.[5] The maximum deviation of the other results from published values are $+9.6\%$ and $-12\%$ .
The average of the 18 positive deviations is $4.9\%$ , and the average of the 12 negative deviations is $-4.4\%$ .

However, Turnbull employed the transient-hot-wire technique with its attendant current-shunting problems (Chapter 2); consequently, the conductivity of HTS cannot be regarded to be as well established as that of the other specimens measured.
The experimental results for HTS at $526^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (Fig.
18) are an example of a test of the theory of Chapter 2 for infrared absorbing materials.

Equation (9) in conjunction with the values of $\overline{\kappa}$ , $\overline{n}$ , $\epsilon$ , and $\kappa$ (selected in the manner previously described) agrees well with the experimental thermal resistance data over the range of specimen thicknesses examined.

However, as Poltz<sup>17</sup> points out, the Kirchoff theory ceases to be valid if the reciprocal of the absorption coefficient is of the same magnitude as the wavelength in some regions of the absorption spectrum.

If we extrapolate our one result for the conductivity of solid HTS at $120^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to the melting point at $142^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by assuming that thermal conductivity is proportional to the inverse of the absolute temperature $\mathrm{k} \propto 1 / \mathrm{T}$ ( $^\circ\mathrm{K}$ ) (according to the Debye phonon-scattering theory), a ratio of the liquid-to-solid thermal conductivity at the melting point is found to be 0.85.
Turnbull<sup>25</sup> reports the average ratio of liquid-to-solid conductivities at the melting point for a large number of salts to be 0.86 ± 0.13.
However, Turnbull's reported values for HTS<sup>5</sup> do not show a discontinuity at the melting point.
Accordingly, our value of the conductivity of solid HTS, 14% higher than Turnbull's, appears to be the more reasonable one.

The effects of the specimen temperature range (300 to $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) and of the degree of radiation effects (zero to maximum radiation at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) do not contribute greatly to the error and are shown by the areas included within the small bands in Fig.

Such a system was not considered necessary for the present apparatus since the conductivities of the molten fluoride salts were found to be in the vicinity of $0.01\ \mathrm{Wcm}^{-1}(^\circ\mathrm{C})^{-1}$ .

Estimated standard and maximum error limits in the conductivity measurements for Apparatus III-B vs specimen conductivities with and without radiative heat transfer over 300 to $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ range.
Figure 22 shows also the deviations of each of the experimental results from the published values.

Since the uncertainty of the temperature measured with the Chromel-Alumel thermocouples is four times of that from Pt vs Pt-10% Rh thermocouples, the error limits for Apparatus I-A are considerably larger than for III-B, especially at the higher specimen conductivities.
Excluding these points, 93% of the data lies within the maximum error limits and 68% within the standard error limits.

Model I-A endured 500 hr of operation with a fluoride salt mixture at temperatures ranging from 500 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and for 850 hr with HTS at temperatures from 200 to $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ before failure of a thermocouple required the replacement of the heater assembly.

Model II-A suffered a weld failure in the heater assembly after 1000 hr of operation with helium and argon at temperatures from 200 to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 800 hr with fluoride salt mixtures from 500 to $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Model III-B is still in operating condition after nearly 3000 hr with fluoride salt mixtures at temperatures from 500 to $960^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Designed as it is for specialized thermal conductivity measurements with molten fluoride salt mixtures from 500 to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the variable-gap apparatus has demonstrated remarkable versatility.

Experimental results agree with published results within an average deviation of $\pm 5\%$ for a wide variety of specimens (solids, liquids, and gases), specimen conductivities $[0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ to $100 \times 10^{-3}$ W cm $^{-1}$ (°C) $^{-1}$ ], and temperature levels (40 to $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

text: The discussion of the methods in Chapter 2 and Appendix C suggests that the geometry of the apparatus exerts considerable effect on the accuracy of the conductivity determination.

section: ADDITIONAL DETAILS OF DESIGN OF THE APPARATUS
text: The most important part of the assembly is the $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ heater core machined from a high-purity, high-density $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ ceramic.

section: ADDITIONAL DETAILS OF DESIGN OF THE APPARATUS
text: The core is fabricated in two pieces and is designed so that a 60-in. length of 0.010-in.-diam Pt- $10\%$ Rh wire could be uniformly wound on each piece forming the main and guard heaters.
